comments.content:
What is the origin (if known) of the visualization of expanding until one is standing on the 1’ diameter sphere of the earth?

Was there a different way of doing the same thing, when people’s concept of the world was based more on their lived experience and less on abstract knowledge that they live on a spherical planet? (I would imagine that, though it has been known for a while that the earth is round, this fact only fairly recently became so prominent in our everyday concept of where we are.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The expansion visualization is apparently quite recent. It's not included in the original version of the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram in the Golden Dawn papers, in Crowley's version in Liber O, or in Butler's The Magician: His Training and Work, which latter was my introduction to the ritual. I find that I'm not sure where I got it -- it's been part of my practice for many decades, but I read a vast amount of magical literature back in the day.

The thing is, you can cultivate the lived experience of being on a spherical planet; it doesn't have to be abstract. Lie on a grassy hill sometime on an early summer evening when the Moon is already up and the evening star is visible, and make an effort to realize that you're not looking up from a floor, you're looking out from the side of a sphere, and the Moon and Venus and the Sun are also spheres, whirling through deep space. Do it with suitable intensity and it won't be abstract any longer...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I was taught a Vajrayana-informed meditation (oral teaching) that involved something very close. It doesn't explicitly say to picture the world as a sphere, but it has you visualize yourself at roughly that scale, a titanically large being (implicitly a Bodhisattva, I think) standing on Earth with your shoulders amidst the heavenly bodies. Given the attention paid in early modern magic to Asian and specifically Tibetan teachings, the idea may have come from there. (Alternatively the Golden Dawn version may have roots in the Kabbalistic 'Adam Kadmon.' Few ideas have only one parent!)

My teacher cautioned that people who have mental health problems involving dissociation, depersonalization, or psychosis should skip this step in the visualization - don't fool around with your perception of physical self if you can't reliably reset it to normal when you're done.

Is this because reconciling the different philosophies and morals of the two cultures is not possible because they are not close enough or that they have not had close enough contact for long enough? Or is that they have not formed a hybrid culture. Do the "Barbarians at the Gates" tend to be the forerunners of magical innovations?

replies.title: Re: Combining natural magic
replies.content: Hmm. I've tried to explain this repeatedly and I'm not sure where the confusion is coming from. The problem is specifically with combining those aspects of East Asian magic that move life energy through the body and those aspects of Western magic that do the same thing, and the difficulty is that combining them can mess with your health. That's the issue. It doesn't apply to the rest of ceremonial magic, as far as I know; it's only, and specifically, that running energy through the body in two competing fashions can cause serious trouble to the subtle body. Thus it has nothing to do with reconciling philosophies or morals, or the length of time the cultures have been in contact; the problem is comparable to the problem you get when you take two medications and their side effects interact in ways that make you sick. Does that clarify things a bit?

replies.title: Re: Combining natural magic
replies.content: Yes. Enormously.

replies.title: Re: Combining natural magic
replies.content:
If I may, I think it would be really useful to many of those present to have a brief post clarifying the following distinctions:

a) 'natural' magic vs. 'ceremonial' magic. People who have not seen many examples of the latter may struggle to intuit which features are characteristic - is it the complexity, the Hebrew, the robes? Without that context they're conceptually in the same place as a young child who knows only that 'doggy' is a four-legged hairy thing, and so doesn't understand why the cat is not 'doggy.'

b) a practice that 'moves energy through the body' vs. one that draws shapes in light on or around the body as in the Kabbalistic Cross. That's a fairly fine distinction for those who are still getting comfortable with how occultism understand 'energy,' or for that matter what constitutes the 'energy body' as distinct from the physical body.

c) a practice that 'invokes spiritual beings' vs. one that vibrates god-names as in the SOP. 'Invoke' is often used in monotheistic contexts to mean 'call on the name of,' and people who haven't experienced e.g. the Afro-Caribbean religions may have no concept of 'invoke' as meaning 'request the immediate local presence of.' Therefore saying "Don't invoke around children, but do the SOP" may appear contradictory.

You've already addressed some of these, but that's been piecemeal, usually brief and in the comments, and often dealing with slightly-atypical examples like banishing rituals (ceremonial but suitable for laypeople, etc.). I suggest that some combination of heuristics ('Ceremonial magic comes as part of an initiatory program' or what-have-you) and examples could help bring things into focus for the perplexed.

replies.title: Re: Combining natural magic
replies.content: Seconded, if I may

replies.title: Re: Combining natural magic
replies.content: That won't be a brief post, but I'll consider it.

replies.title: Re: Astrological question
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, my take these days is that Uranus rules Aquarius. Where's your Uranus placement, and how does it relate to the other planets in your natal chart?

Second of all, you're probably not going to find the information you want online; most online astrology sites are very shallow. Pick up any good introductory book on astrology -- my personal fave is Llewellyn George's A-Z Horoscope Maker and Delineator (not The New A-Z etc., which has been messed with) -- and look up, in order, Aquarius on the 2nd house cusp; your Uranus placement; and the various aspects Uranus makes with other planets. Write down what each entry says -- unless you have a phenomenal memory, you'll lose too much if you don't do this -- and then take some time to work out a combined description of the effects. (This is called "synthesis," and it's a basic astrological skill.)

You'll notice that this is going to take you probably an hour or so of close attention and serious thinking. That's why I don't do astrological readings on my journal for free. ;-)

comments.content: You said you were very impressed with the results of the Masons' and other fraternal groups' youth training programmes. How do these programmes work and are occult methods part of them?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, occult methods aren't part of them! The youth groups I've seen use a far more straightforward method. The essence of it is that each such group has a colorful initiation ritual that the kids themselves perform, and each such group also manages its own affairs via elected officers under adult supervision. So each boy or girl who gets involved in such an organization has a chance to learn how to speak in public, how to handle basic organizational politics, how to handle the responsibility of an elected position, how to make commitments and stick to them, and how to interact constructively with other kids in a variety of situations, some of them stressful. Half a dozen years spent doing those things is enormously beneficial to any young person who can handle social interactions at all, and leaves them much better prepared to deal with adult life than the kids who never get such experiences.

comments.title: Witchcraft
comments.content:
Hello!

What is your understanding of witchcraft and its practices? And what are the major differences between that and magic as practiced by druids and all?

PS: I am aware that these could be far too general questions, but try your best.

Karim

replies.title: Re: Witchcraft
replies.content:
Okay, to begin with, the word "witchcraft" means two completely different things. First, it means the folk magic practiced among European and European-diaspora working class communities from the late Middle Ages through the Victorian era. (Call this W1.) Second, it means a new religious movement that was launched by Gerald Gardner in the late 1940s and has proliferated in various directions since then, not all of them directly descended from Gardner's work. (Call this W2.) The new religious movement originally claimed that these two things were the same thing, but every assessment of the evidence by people who don't have a vested interest in that claim has shown that there's basically no connection at all.

W1, witchcraft as it was practiced by working class folk magicians in the European world, was a mixture of local folk traditions with medieval and Renaissance high magic: it's indicative that for three centuries, the most popular book used by English witches was the English translation of Cornelius Agrippa's Three Books of Occult Philosophy, a textbook of Renaissance high magic. W1 was also explicitly Christian -- witches used Christian and Jewish names of God to do their magic, and there's precisely zero evidence of the worship of non-Christian deities in those social strata at that time. (Pagan worship was, as it remained until very recently, an interest of well-off intellectuals.) There are plenty of good books on W1; one that I particularly like is Jim Baker's The Cunning Man's Handbook: The Practice of English Folk Magic 1550-1900.

W2 is very widespread just now, but it's never greatly interested me and you'd be better off asking someone who does it. As for its relation to Druidry, that depends on the witch, and also on the Druid! I know some Druids whose practice is strongly influenced by W2, and others (I'm one of these latter) who don't use W2 at all.

replies.title: Re: Witchcraft
replies.content:
There is also a "W3," so to speak, though it is little known to scholars, or for that matter, to practicing witches nowadays.

From the 1840s onward, when Mesmerism and then Spiritualism were all the rage, there was a popular theory in the United States that witches and magicians of bygone ages had been doing exactly the same things, and using he same abilities, that Mesmerists and Spiritualist mediums were doing and using then. (This theory was also exported to Great Britain a few decades later.) Mesmeric operators were always few in number, but from the 1860s onward active Spiritualist mediums numbered in the high hundreds, if not the low thousands.

By the later 1860s some influential women Spiritualist mediums were actually claiming the word "witch" as an empowering label for themselves, and towards the end of the 1800s one can even find occasional news items in serious media about actual schools that were offering public lessons in witchcraft to all seekers.

By then, people had begun to notice that bygone witches were sometimes said to meet in groups of about 13 under a leader's direction, and did things in a circle. Since Spiritualists also held their most powerful seances around a circular table, and the ideal number of participants in such a circle was six men, six women, and a Medium (13 in all), it was assumed (without documentation) that some of the other things Spiritualists did in their circular seances -- raise power, pass it around the circle, and contain it therein, for example -- had also been done by witches in bygone times.

Some of these Mediums who occasionally called themselves witches had also begun to look into earlier printed handbooks of ceremonial magic, e.g. pseudo-Agrippa's Fourth Book and the similar work attributed to Peter of Abano (Apono).

Out of this milieu came at least a few pre-Gardnerian groups, usually consisting just of family and close friends, who ended up deciding that, yes, they actually were witches, and their private groups were real covens. Since Spiritualism had been considered a new religion by most Spiritualists since its beginnings, what these witches did in their covens was also regarded as a religion, namely, the religion of Witchcraft.

The best documented of these groups that I know of was the Michigan one centered around the mother and grandmother of Marion Koclo, also known as Gundella, the Green Witch. But there certainly were other such groups as well.

Since Gerald Gardner had also been intensely interested in Spiritualism before he started to publish as a witch, I have no doubt that he was somewhat influenced by this "W3" as he developed and publicized his own W2. Of course, many other things influenced him as well, not the least of whom were such magicians as William Seabrook and Aleister Crowley. But that's another part of a very complicated story.

replies.title: Re: Witchcraft 3
replies.content:
This is really interesting. Do you have any sources to cite for followup? Years ago I had a correspondent who claimed a family tradition that sounded much more like this sort of thing--meeting in street clothes, sitting in circle with joined hands. I never got much detail from him and probably don't have the letters anymore. Seem to recall he was in Midwest? Anyhow, I would like to know more. There is so much more to this question than "we have been doing this since the Cave bears" vs. GG made it all up in the 1950s.

Rita R

replies.title: Re: Witchcraft 3
replies.content:
Alas, Rita, I don't have a whole lot of hard information on other groups in the Midwest than Gundella's.

Gundella was quite good friends with a sociologist at Eastern Michigan University, Marcello Truzzi, who published a few papers about modern Witchcraft groups in the late '60s and early '70s. She herself published a pair of books about ghosts and ghost-hunting in Michigan, with prefaces by Truzzi, which also contained appendices on some of her Witchcraft practices--chiefly on how to conduct séances! (Gundella also worked as a consulting Witch, and left Truzzi her extensive casebooks after her death. They are now preserved with Truzzi's own files in the archives of his university.) She also wrote a few things about her own family background in witchcraft, which are just enough to let a genealogical researcher determine precisely which line of her maternal descent was the one in which the women thought of themselves as Witches.

If your correspondent hailed from Michigan, his group might be connected with Gundella's family. If not, it's clear that Guhdella's family was not an isolated, unusual case, so one might look for some other such family in his background.

There were lots of families in the USA which cultivated esoteric interests down through the generations, just as there were lots and lots of families which bnelonged to Swedenborgian Churches, or participated in, say, Freemasonry or the Grange or some other fraternal tradition, down through many generations. One can speak of "esoteric families" just as one can speak of "Swedenborgian families" or "Masonic families" or "Grange families."

(My own family, on my mother's side, was such an "esoteric family" at least since the days of my great-great-grandmother, who died a very old woman in the first decade of the 1900s. It was my curiosity about my family's own peculiarities that got me started researching these things about thirty years ago.)

If you want to look into the background of "W3" a little more deeply, two very good places to start are books by (1) Alan Putnam of Salem (yes, that Salem), especially his _Witchcraft of New England Explained by Modern Spiritualism_, and (2) Emma Hardinge Britten, especially her lecture "Ancient Magic and Modern Spiritualism" in her_Extemporaneous Addresses_ and her _Art Magic_ and _Ghost Land_. (You can find PDFs of all of them on the web.)

For Emma Hardinge Britten you could also look at my own small account of her life and esoteric interests, _The Unseen Worlds of Emma Hardinge Britten: Some Chapters in the History of Western Occultism_ (2001). You can find a PDF on line, on my page at academia.edu.

PS: On the West Coast, I suspect the Harpy Coven of Victor Anderson's early years has this sort of origin in Spiritualism, though I haven't yet got any documentation for or against. (The enigmatic Central Valley Tradition of Witchcraft, however, probably descends from a Gardnerian woman who was an exchange teacher in Berkeley High School during the school year 1961/62. I'm not done working on that puzzle.)

replies.content:

comments.title: Watcher on the threshold

Thank you for magic Monday.

Dion Fortune wrote in Mystical Kabbalah ch 22: “This brings us to the great question, which might almost be called the Dweller on the Threshold of occult science, the horror which confronts every adventurer into the Unseen which unites in itself the functions of the Sphinx, and asks a question of the soul upon the answer to which hangs his fate. replies.title: Re: Watcher on the threshold
replies.content: You have to make that decision yourself. To borrow a turn of phrase from a once-iconic book, fear is the first enemy of a person of knowledge. That fear is the Watcher. If you overcome it, you begin to walk the path.

comments.title: Deity names
comments.content:
Dear JMG, I have a question about the use of the various names of deities in magical rituals and prayers. For instance Hu Gadarn can be Hu the mighty or Heu’c. I’m sure there are more variants and of course the ones originally used in pre-christian times are likely to be different again.
My question is does any of this matter and when one calls on a deity how specific is this call? Or to put it another way are Hermes, Mercury and Wotan all the same power, variations of the same power, or individual powers? Are Ceridwen and the Virgin Mary both the same mother goddess or different aspects of one goddess, or quite separate?
If the specific name or variant of a name is not that important how does one decide which to use?
I’m probably over thinking this as in practice it all seems to ‘work’ but it’s still a nagging intellectual issue for me.

replies.title: Re: Deity names
replies.content:
The thing is, we don't know. All of human knowledge can be reduced to statements on the order of "when we do X, Y generally happens, and we have a crude mental model that we use to explain it to ourselves."

In practical terms, the choice of a name depends on a range of factors. Heu'c is used in the Druidical LBRP because that needs names of four letters. I'd invoke Ceridwen rather than the Virgin Mary because I have no initiatory connection with the latter (I was never baptized) while my main spiritual tradition works quite a bit with the former. And I'd call on Hermes or Mercury for help with an alchemical working, rather than Wotan, because the latter isn't traditionally associated with alchemy and the two former are.

replies.title: Re: Initiation
replies.content:
(A) there's no need to analyze everything unless you want to.

(B) it's an important part of this initiation that it's not public. Yes, there are some kinds of initiation that are about redefining your relationship to other people, but this is not that kind of initiation. This initiation is about redefining your relationship to yourself and to the spiritual realms, and it's important to the work that it's not a public event, that it's entirely private to you and the sacred powers with whom you work, and that it defines part of your life that is shared only by you and those who have done the same experience themselves.

comments.title: Magical Battle of Britain
comments.content:
Hi, JMG. I’ve recently been reading The Magical Battle of Britain, which I find absolutely fascinating. But while reading it one question has come to my mind: while British mages, under the leadership of Dion Fortune, were able to tap into the centuries-old cultural images (e.g., associated with the legend of King Arthur) to strengthen and unify the British “race”, I wonder how, or if, such a thing would be possible in colonial countries (e.g., USA, Canada, Australia, New Zealand), where the ancient cultural images are Indigenous (and not known to the majority of the population) and the “newcomers” have not been in these countries long enough to have created deeply-held cultural images that are unique to the colonial country in question. Perhaps I am wrong re: the USA, having had a distinct identity longer than the other countries listed above -- but I am not American and cannot really say.

So, I guess my question is: if a colonial country’s very existence is threatened by a foreign power, would the technique used by Fortune and company likely work? If not, could some other working be produced that would likely have a similarly powerful effect? I know this question is terribly hypothetical, but I figure it is worth a shot!

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Magical Battle of Britain
replies.content:
Nope. The Matter of Britain is specific to the land of Britain. Each region of the Earth's surface has its own spiritual dimension, which doesn't transplant. Part of why colonial countries like yours and mine tend to end up in various kinds of messes is that the European colonists, being Christians, didn't learn about the sacred places and energies of the new land on which they settled -- quite the contrary -- and so we have no roots into the deep places of the land, and no access to the kind of energies Fortune could call on. That'll resolve itself eventually, but a lot of time will pass and a lot of blood will be spilled first.

If your culture has emotionally evocative images that relate strongly to its collective identity, it might be possible to do a working with those; here in the US, for example, the Statue of Liberty is such an image. If not, you're out of luck.

replies.title: Re: Magical Battle of Britain
replies.content: OK, I'll have to say I'm confused...if each region has it's own spiritual dimensions, and they don't transplant, how does something like druidism 'work' in the US?

replies.title: Re: Magical Battle of Britain
replies.content: To say that you can't call on the deep energies of the land doesn't mean that you can't call on any energies at all!

replies.title: Re: Magical Battle of Britain
replies.content:
For what it's worth, having lived in different parts of the country, I feel that this is a bit different in different places. The American culture of the Pacific Northwest and Southern California feels to me like a very thin and very delicate veneer; the deeper culture is Indigenous and, in SoCal, Mexican. On the other hand, on the East Coast, I feel that the American spirit is rooted much more deeply in the landscape. That may be only in comparison with the West Coast, which is basically not American at all; I haven't been to Europe and I don't know how it feels there. But I can imagine a Magical Battle of Boston (say) effectively invoking the power of the Minutemen and the Founding Fathers.

Does your experience of New England bear this out at all?

replies.title: Re: Magical Battle of Britain
replies.content:
This jives very well with my feelings, too. I live in the PNW on the Canada side, and it is a thin veneer, which is why I think indigenous-led movements are the way social adaptation to descent will/must happen here. But back in Cheticamp, and even Halifax, Nova Scotia, there is a "bothness" sense. Like the settlers there had formed more relationship with the land, and some of their European nature spirits or godforms had taken up shop as well. I don't know personally, but hear, that it is similar in Newfoundland.

I wonder how much had to do with settlement occurring back when most people still had intimate work with land and sea to live, whereas by the time settlement out here got going, industrialization was well along - I.e. if it's not just time - young culture transplants further north up the coast in the old Japanese fishing villages might have also developed a more powerful spiritual relationship with the land already, I wonder what Japanese spirit may have soaked in up there?

replies.title: Re: Magical Battle of Britain
replies.content: Putting JMG's interpretation in history, Southern Californian deeper culture shouldn't be "Mexican" in any sense: the Castillan speakers there were only a little older than the English speakers, and the Amerindians there had only indirect trade with Mesoamericans of any kind, so were in no way "Mexican". Putting an interpretation that grants a measure of Englishness to New England, Southern Californian deep culture would be part-Castillian, and much more connected to Mexico through that than through anything Amerindian.

replies.title: Re: Magical Battle of Britain
replies.content:
FWIW, my standing impression is that there's at least one big internal division of the North American landscape (at minimum, Mesoamerica is distinct from North America); that may be affecting how well the American spirit roots, though sheer duration of settlement and the bloodshed of the Revolutionary War/(first) American Civil War probably also has an effect. (I'm pretty sure at least one of the deep cultures on the continent pushes towards a circular/fractal worldview that meshes poorly with the linear Faustian one, adding to the fun.)

The South is in a particularly awkward spot wrt the land, I think: it's rooted, but the land really isn't happy about that. Think mistletoe on a tree. That's not going to end well.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Try making the first few the situation, the second one you, and the third one the outcome; that works well for me. Also, you've only been practicing it for a few weeks, and unless you're much more talented than most people, that's not long enough to start getting clear answers.

2) You'll have to decide if you want to follow it up or not; that's important work, but whether it's your work or not is another question. The major dangers are (a) becoming a religious fanatic, and trying to force your deity down everyone else's throat, and (b) losing your grip on material reality and needing institutionalization. Those can be avoided by maintaining what Philip Carr-Gomm calls the three mystic senses of Druidry: common sense, a sense of proportion, and a sense of humor. Above all else, if you find yourself thinking of yourself as special, holy, called to some grandiose destiny or other, or the like, run the other direction as fast as you can; that way lies madness. (I mean this quite literally. I've seen people go down this road and end up with a fine case of florid, violent paranoid schizophrenia with a strong megalomaniac component.) Beyond that? Don't take up the work unless you're prepared to do it for the rest of your life.

replies.title: Researching Ogham fews
replies.content:
I have recently begun daily Ogham few divinations for my Druid Magic study, just the basic three-few reading JMG mentions of Situation, Me, and Outcome, and I have found it helpful to research the actual properties of the trees.

For instance, Phagos has popped up for me three times now, so the first time it did, I looked up the tree it is associated with, beech, and discovered beech comes from a root word meaning "book" in several languages and was used for writing tablets by ancient Germanic societies before the advent of paper. Also, the wood is slow and steady-burning. What this meant for me personally is that Phagos symbolizes the value of gaining knowledge via perseverance.

Phagos first showed when I began to study some gods and goddesses for the SoP. The second time, it was ill-dignified because I was feeling unsure about the accuracy of my Ogham readings, so I looked at the few next to it, which was Quert, meaning healing and recovery, which to me seemed like a clear message to just keep working on it. The next time I pulled it was after a productive discursive meditation on the various meanings and implications of the word "sacrifice". Hope this helps.

By re-doing, I mean having the sabbats match the planting rhythm here, as I am not harvesting much of anything at Lammas, and it is too hot for bread anyway. It doesn't start noticeably cooling off until Mabon (equinox), though still too warm to slaughter any large livestock until January most years. replies.title: Re: different seasons
replies.content:
I'll answer from a general Wiccan perspective (my own). Ur-Wicca started with the four Celtic festivals (the cross-quarter days), then added the solstices, and finally the equinoxes. FWIW, there is some reason to think that additional seasonal holidays were added to provide additional occasions to get together and have a party. I don't know who coined the term "Wheel of the Year". The only semi-relevatory text we agree on says nought directly about sabbats; it says "better it be when the moon is full."

Many Wiccans adjust their seasonal observances to local conditions. If you see a need for a major overhaul, I would suggest thinking about and if possible, discussing with others of your tradition, the reasons why you are performing these rituals in the first place. There are a number of possible reasons, not mutually exclusive. E.g. gathering to experience and celebrate the ties of community, family and friends. Feeling our connections with the land, other living things, the sun and rain on which our life depends, etc. Blessing and protecting the land, plants, animals and the people and/or acknowledging the blessings and protection received from the land, plants, animals and the gods. Connecting with and honoring ancestors and teachers who have gone before. Enacting in symbolic form, in order to experience and partake of, the divine mysteries of your particular tradition.

If you talk with other local witches about what the seasonal markers are of your place, your psychic senses of shifting tides, what you want to do most when the seasons change, then what you find in common may be incorporated into your sabbats either directly or symbolically or narratively. You might not need eight sabbats. They might cluster differently on the calendar. They almost certainly will have a different feel to them than if you were doing them farther north. OTOH, there is value in observing at least some sabbats on the traditional dates with the traditional acts so you can connect to the magical current of other witches who are meeting at roughly the same time.

If you need new rituals or additions to the old ones, start with something specific that speaks to the non-rational brain or the subconscious: an action, a symbol or image, a song, a poem, a dance.

I hope this is useful, but in any case, I hope others will offer ideas.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The First World War was a massive crisis of confidence for the Western world, much more so than most people realize. Up until the outbreak of war, most people believed as a matter of course that war between the great powers was a thing of the past, that Western Europe was the cutting edge of progress, and that everything would just get better and better -- very much the sort of thing Stephen Pinker is spouting nowadays. And then war came, and it became brutally clear that the various High Commands had no idea how to win, and the body count mounted up, and up, and up, with tens of thousands of lives being snuffed out to gain a few square miles of bloodsoaked mud...and it just kept on going.

When the war finally ended, one consequence was an immense revulsion for the established assumptions of European society. There was a drastic cultural break, and it hit religion among many other things; the mainstream churches embraced various avant-garde movements such as the Oxford Group, setting the stage for their post-World War Two plunge into irrelevance, and old-fashioned practices such as discursive meditation went into the trash heap. The result was a century of nearly complete cultural incoherence, from which we haven't yet recovered.

replies.title: Re: Magic with children in the house, magical ethics
replies.content:
(1 & 2) When you have young children in the house, it's considered unsafe to do the kind of high-end ceremonial magic that involves bringing spiritual energies into manifestation. You can do all the basic practices with perfect safety -- banishing rituals, the Middle Pillar exercise, meditation, and divination -- though it's good not to have young children in the same room while you're practicing. Until they're three or so, don't do the major magical workings in the same house where they live; after that, you can do it when they're out of the house. Once they're seven or so you're fine.

(3) I don't know of a book on magical ethics, and I doubt one would sell well! The basic thing to keep in mind is that if it's unethical to do something when magic's not involved, it's unethical to do it with magic. Always ask someone's permission before doing magic on their behalf, and remember that whatever energy you invoke, for whatever purpose, will manifest in your own life as well.

comments.title: Reporting results on Screen-glamour countermeasures
comments.content:
Hi JMG, CRPatiño here.

These are my observations on my first glamour breaking experiment. Let's try to get it organized.

The setup: I have used half a dozen of weath-grain-needles (tiny and very sharp, normally used in the face for cosmetic acupuncture) and a few stapples (less small, less sharp) to increase the bulk of iron. On furhter thought I added one 1.5" nail (biggest of all, pointy but not sharp at all). No spells, enchantments or invocations were used, since I intended to test the bare magic properties of the metal itself. This was hidden in the base of a flat screen TV set used almost exclusively by my children (ages 15 and 10, both male). No explanation at all was given to the "test subjects" that an experiment was being carried on.

Initial hypothesis. The natural properties of metal will break the glamour-like effects of that TV set in particular, so the boys will spend less time using it. However, this will not break the effect of other devices at home, so it is also possible (even likely) that they will switch to use different devices for entertainment.

Results: Inconclusive. I observed a strong effect on the first day, then a regression to the mean over the next week. The effect was stronger in the youngest who demanded more attention for the family to play offscreen and then went to bed earlier (it's a bit of a home issue to have him turn off the TV and get in the shower, which then put pressure in the bed time. The complet opposite of that happened on the first night).
Now-a-days, they seem to be using the screen about as much as ever, but the pattern is different. One of the persistent effects I do observe is that I no longer catch the trance-like gaze they used to had when looking at the screen (though this is a subjective measure, so you must suspect bias from the part of the reporter); as a matter of fact, it is more or less common that they turn on the device but then go on to do something else (either read, do crafts, or even use a handheld screen!!!) at the same time, effectively using the TV as a radio. This does not happen when they play a videogame on the screen, since that is more engaging and demands their attention.

Conclusions: My initial expectations were offbase, but some effect is still observable. In particular, my take is that I am not modelling the glamour thing accurately. I was thinking in terms of a "scientific" energy field, like in the electromagnetic spectrum, that would get jammed by the metal. In retrospective, it looks more like a living thing, that is capable of adaptation after an initial stimuli. Suggestions are welcome for further experimentation.

replies.title: Re: Reporting results on Screen-glamour countermeasures
replies.content: Many thanks for the data points!
